core_meaning: <ruby>大丈夫<rt>だいじょうぶ</rt></ruby> covers a wide range from 'I'm okay / it's fine' to 'no problem / don't worry' to 'will this be alright?' — it expresses that something is safe, acceptable, or not a problem. It can be a reassurance, a check-in question, or even a polite way to decline an offer.

sentences.jp: <ruby>転<rt>こ</rt></ruby>んじゃったけど、<ruby>大丈夫<rt>だいじょうぶ</rt></ruby>だよ。
sentences.notes: The most basic use — reassuring someone that you're physically fine. <ruby>転<rt>こ</rt></ruby>んじゃった is a natural casual contraction of <ruby>転<rt>こ</rt></ruby>んでしまった.

sentences.jp: このまま<ruby>進<rt>すす</rt></ruby>めて<ruby>大丈夫<rt>だいじょうぶ</rt></ruby>ですか？
sentences.translation: Is it okay if we proceed like this?
sentences.notes: Used as a question to check whether something is acceptable or safe — a very common function of <ruby>大丈夫<rt>だいじょうぶ</rt></ruby> in everyday situations.

sentences.jp: 「コーヒーはいかがですか？」「あ、<ruby>大丈夫<rt>だいじょうぶ</rt></ruby>です。」
sentences.translation: 'Would you like some coffee?' 'Oh, I'm fine, thanks.'
sentences.notes: <ruby>大丈夫<rt>だいじょうぶ</rt></ruby>です is frequently used as a soft refusal of an offer — a nuance that surprises many learners who expect it to mean only 'I'm okay.'

sentences.jp: <ruby>明日<rt>あした</rt></ruby>までに<ruby>終<rt>お</rt></ruby>わる？——うん、<ruby>大丈夫<rt>だいじょうぶ</rt></ruby>、もうほとんどできてるから。
sentences.translation: 'Can you finish by tomorrow?' 'Yeah, it's fine — I'm almost done.'
sentences.notes: Here <ruby>大丈夫<rt>だいじょうぶ</rt></ruby> means 'no problem / it'll be fine' — a confident reassurance about a future outcome.

sentences.jp: <ruby>患者<rt>かんじゃ</rt></ruby>の<ruby>状態<rt>じょうたい</rt></ruby>は<ruby>現在<rt>げんざい</rt></ruby><ruby>大丈夫<rt>だいじょうぶ</rt></ruby>ですが、<ruby>経過<rt>けいか</rt></ruby>を<ruby>観察<rt>かんさつ</rt></ruby>する<ruby>必要<rt>ひつよう</rt></ruby>があります。
sentences.translation: The patient's condition is currently stable, but we need to monitor their progress.
sentences.notes: Even in formal/professional contexts <ruby>大丈夫<rt>だいじょうぶ</rt></ruby> appears naturally, though some speakers prefer <ruby>問題<rt>もんだい</rt></ruby>ありません in very stiff official writing.

dont_use: Avoid using <ruby>大丈夫<rt>だいじょうぶ</rt></ruby> in very stiff official documents or bureaucratic writing — <ruby>問題<rt>もんだい</rt></ruby>ございません or <ruby>支障<rt>ししょう</rt></ruby>はありません is more appropriate there. Also be careful: saying <ruby>大丈夫<rt>だいじょうぶ</rt></ruby>です in response to an offer can come across as a refusal even if you meant to accept — if you want to say 'yes please,' use いただきます or はい、ぜひ instead. Don't confuse the declining nuance with rudeness; it's perfectly polite, just easy to misread cross-culturally.

confused_with.contrast: A learner might reach for <ruby>問題<rt>もんだい</rt></ruby>ない in casual conversation — 'それ、<ruby>問題<rt>もんだい</rt></ruby>ない？' — when <ruby>大丈夫<rt>だいじょうぶ</rt></ruby>？ would sound far more natural between friends. <ruby>問題<rt>もんだい</rt></ruby>ない is perfectly correct but feels slightly analytical or businesslike, whereas <ruby>大丈夫<rt>だいじょうぶ</rt></ruby> is the everyday go-to for checking on people or situations. Use <ruby>大丈夫<rt>だいじょうぶ</rt></ruby> in daily speech and save <ruby>問題<rt>もんだい</rt></ruby>ない / <ruby>問題<rt>もんだい</rt></ruby>ありません for when you're deliberately framing things in terms of 'no issues exist.'

frequency: <ruby>大丈夫<rt>だいじょうぶ</rt></ruby> is extremely common in everyday spoken Japanese across all age groups — you'll hear it dozens of times a day in casual conversation, service interactions, and workplaces. It is also common in informal writing (texts, social media) but slightly less dominant in formal written Japanese.
anki_hint: <ruby>大丈夫<rt>だいじょうぶ</rt></ruby> has three faces: a reassurance ('I'm fine'), a check ('is this okay?'), and a soft refusal ('no thanks') — the refusal use is the one that catches learners off guard.
